<!-- Topic 4: Passing to Functions -->
<!-- Total slides: 6 -->

# Passing to Functions

---

## How does each type behave as a function argument? {.smaller}

- `int[]` decays to a pointer — size information is lost
- `std::array` and `vector` carry their size with them

::: notes
Total slides: 6
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Passing int[]

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

void print(int arr[], int size);

int main() {
    int scores[5] = {91, 78, 84, 95, 62};
    print(scores, 5);
    return 0;
}

void print(int arr[], int size) {
    for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
        cout << arr[i] << "\n";
    }
}
```

::: notes
int arr[] in a parameter list is identical to int* arr — the array has already decayed. Changes inside the function affect the caller's array. Size must be passed separately every time.
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Passing std::array

```{.cpp}
#include <array>
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

void print(const array<int, 5>& arr);

int main() {
    array<int, 5> scores = {91, 78, 84, 95, 62};
    print(scores);
    return 0;
}

void print(const array<int, 5>& arr) {
    for (int s : arr) {
        cout << s << "\n";
    }
}
```

::: notes
No size parameter. const & avoids copying the array and prevents modification. The size is baked into the type, so the compiler enforces it.
:::

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Passing vector

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
using namespace std;

void print(const vector<int>& v);

int main() {
    vector<int> scores = {91, 78, 84, 95, 62};
    print(scores);
    return 0;
}

void print(const vector<int>& v) {
    for (int s : v) {
        cout << s << "\n";
    }
}
```

::: notes
const vector<int>& avoids copying and prevents modification. v.size() is available inside the function. Works for any vector size — not locked to a fixed N.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Comparison

| Type | Parameter syntax | Size inside function |
|---|---|---|
| `int[]` | `int arr[], int size` | Via `size` parameter |
| `array<T,N>` | `const array<T,N>& arr` | Via `.size()` |
| `vector<T>` | `const vector<T>& v` | Via `.size()` |

- `int[]` requires a separate size parameter every call
- `std::array` locks the size in the type — only one size works
- `vector` is the most flexible — any size, safe, STL-ready

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Summary

- `int[]` decays to a pointer when passed — always pair it with a size parameter
- Pass `std::array` and `vector` by `const &`; both carry their size and protect the caller's data

<!-- Slide 6 -->
